food_name: Beef Ball Noodle Soup (Hu Tieu Bo Vien)
aliases: rice noodle soup with beef balls

english: rice noodle soup with beef balls

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese beef ball noodle soup (Hu Tieu Bo Vien). The closest defensible FoodOn anchor is the generic noodle soup food product class (FOODON_00002473). A more specific term is not available in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_description: Soups and broths — noodle soup (composite dish)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (soups and broths) is the closest defensible category for a broth-based noodle soup dish. No dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for Hu Tieu Bo Vien specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to this specific Vietnamese noodle soup dish.

main_ingredients: fresh herbs (bean sprouts, coriander, Thai basil)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Beef Ball Noodle Soup (Hu Tieu Bo Vien) weighs approximately 500 g and includes rice noodles, several beef balls, clear broth, and fresh herb garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Nutrient Composition of Food: Beef Ball Noodle Soup / Noodle Soup with Meat Balls (composite dish estimate)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists for Hu Tieu Bo Vien by name. Values are adapted from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for beef ball noodle soup / noodle soup with meatballs (composite dish), which is the closest approved-source equivalent. The input record values (energy 90 kcal, protein 6 g, fat 3 g, carbohydrate 9 g, sodium 360 mg per 100 g) are consistent with HPB Foodo data for this dish category and have been retained. Micronutrient values (calcium 10 mg, iron 0.6 mg, cholesterol 14 mg, saturated fat 1.2 g, trans fat 0.04 g, vitamin C 1 mg) are also consistent with HPB Foodo composite dish data. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several vitamins/minerals are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish category and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Rice noodles (analogous estimate for hu tieu rice noodle base)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for Hu Tieu Bo Vien as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 61 is estimated using the GI of rice noodles (GI ~61, medium category) as the primary carbohydrate source, consistent with published values in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) and the University of Sydney GI Database. The protein from beef balls (6 g/100 g) and the broth matrix moderately attenuate the glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (61 × 45) / 100 = 27.5, where carbohydrate per 500 g serving = 9 g/100 g × 5 = 45 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 360 mg per 100 g; a 500 g bowl delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and salt during preparation, ask for broth on the side to control sodium intake, and avoid consuming the full soup broth to limit sodium to a safer level per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice noodles carry a medium GI (~61) and a full 500 g bowl delivers approximately 45 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~27.5, which is high per serving despite a moderate per-100 g carbohydrate content.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should request a smaller noodle portion with extra broth and vegetables to reduce the glycemic load per serving, and pair the dish with adequate protein from beef balls to moderate the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 360 mg/100 g (1800 mg per bowl) and saturated fat at 1.2 g/100 g (6 g per bowl) from processed beef balls contribute to cardiovascular risk; trans fat is present at 0.04 g/100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake, avoid drinking the full broth, and consider substituting beef balls with lower-fat protein options such as tofu or fish balls to reduce saturated fat and trans fat exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Processed beef balls are high in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory pathways; sodium at 360 mg/100 g approaches the threshold that worsens RA-related tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting beef balls with tofu or fish balls to reduce pro-inflammatory omega-6 load, and should avoid drinking the full broth to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Ball Noodle Soup (Hu Tieu Bo Vien) is a Vietnamese noodle soup dish widely served in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. The dish consists of smooth, springy rice noodles (hu tieu) submerged in a clear, savoury beef bone broth that is slow-simmered with aromatics including garlic, shallots, and dried squid or daikon for depth. The defining protein component is bo vien — firm, bouncy beef balls made from finely pounded beef mixed with fish sauce, starch, and seasoning, then boiled until cooked through. The soup is typically garnished with spring onions, fried shallots, fresh coriander, bean sprouts, and sliced fresh chilli, with fish sauce and hoisin sauce served on the side. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 90 kcal, 6 g protein, 3 g fat (1.2 g saturated), 9 g carbohydrate, 1 g sugar, 0.6 g dietary fibre, and 360 mg sodium. The sodium content is notably high due to the broth, fish sauce, and seasoning, making it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The medium glycemic index (~61) from rice noodles and a glycemic load of ~27.5 per 500 g bowl require portion control for type 2 diabetes patients. The dish is dairy-free and culturally significant as a Vietnamese street food staple that has been adopted into Singapore's multicultural hawker food landscape.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore Foodo database for the closest equivalent composite dish (beef ball noodle soup / noodle soup with meatballs). No exact HPB Foodo entry exists under the Vietnamese name Hu Tieu Bo Vien. The per-100 g values from the input record are consistent with HPB Foodo data for this dish category and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on the rice noodle GI from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, mineral panel, B-vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese noodle soup dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 2. No core macro is zero; all four core macros (energy 90 kcal, protein 6 g, fat 3 g, carbohydrate 9 g) are positive and consistent with the dish composition. 3. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on rice noodles as the primary carbohydrate source (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). 4. The dish is not certified halal; halal status depends on the specific vendor and preparation. 5. Aliases consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
